name: technical-decision-record
description: Use when making technical decisions, choosing technologies, or documenting architectural choices. Creates ADRs (Architecture Decision Records).
capabilities: Guides users through a structured ADR creation process with 5 steps
Provides a markdown template with standard ADR sections
Helps identify constraints including technical, business, and compliance factors
Documents options with pros, cons, and estimated costs
Records consequences including positive, negative, risks, and reversibility
Identifies common mistakes in technical decision-making
author: ChaiWithJai
securitySummary: This is a pure documentation skill containing ADR templates and guidance for documenting technical decisions. No code execution, network calls, or cryptographic operations exist. All static findings are false positives: the scanner incorrectly flagged JSON metadata URLs as network risks, markdown code fences as shell execution, and documentation text about architectural decisions as cryptographic patterns. Safe for publication.
valueStatement: Technical teams often lose context when decisions are made informally. This skill creates structured Architecture Decision Records that capture the problem, constraints, options considered, and consequences for future reference.
limitations: Does not evaluate technical options or provide recommendations
Does not integrate with project management tools
Does not track ADR status or link related decisions automatically
Does not generate diagrams or visualizations

useCases.targetUser: Technical leads and architects evaluating new technologies
useCases.title: New Technology Selection
useCases.description: Document the evaluation process when choosing between React, Vue, or Angular for a new frontend project, capturing all constraints and trade-offs.

useCases.title: Architecture Migration Planning
useCases.description: Record the decision to migrate from a monolith to microservices, including the rationale, risks, and rollback plan.

useCases.title: Process Change Documentation
useCases.description: Formally document a new code review process or testing strategy with clear context and expected outcomes.

promptTemplates.title: Basic ADR Request
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to create an ADR for [DECISION TOPIC]. Help me document this technical decision with the problem, constraints, options, and consequences.

promptTemplates.title: Technology Evaluation
promptTemplates.prompt: We're deciding between [OPTION A], [OPTION B], and [OPTION C] for [USE CASE]. Create an ADR that captures our constraints, evaluates each option with pros and cons, and documents the final decision with reasoning.

promptTemplates.title: Architecture Change Request
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to document our decision to [ARCHITECTURAL CHANGE]. Include the trigger for this change, the risks involved, and a reversibility assessment. Use the full ADR template.

promptTemplates.title: Superseding Previous ADR
promptTemplates.prompt: ADR-[NUMBER] needs to be superseded because [REASON]. Create a new ADR that references the original, explains what changed, and provides updated context and recommendations.
promptTemplates.scenario: Updating a previous decision with new information

bestPractices: Always list at least 2 options, even if one is 'do nothing', to ensure decisions are justified
Match deliberation time to reversibility - spend more effort on irreversible decisions
Include all stakeholders in the Deciders field and get explicit sign-off before finalizing
antiPatterns: Making decisions without exploring alternatives - you cannot justify a choice without knowing what you chose against
Editing old ADRs when context changes - this loses the historical record; instead create a new ADR that supersedes the old one
Making decisions that affect teams without involving them - this creates surprise, resistance, and rework

faq.question: What is an ADR?
faq.answer: An ADR (Architecture Decision Record) is a document that captures important technical decisions, the context behind them, and the reasoning for the chosen approach. It serves as a reference for future team members.

faq.question: When should I create an ADR?
faq.answer: Create an ADR when making technology choices, architectural changes, selecting third-party services, changing established patterns, or any decision you want to reference later.

faq.question: How detailed should an ADR be?
faq.answer: Match detail to reversibility. Low-stakes reversible decisions need less detail. High-stakes irreversible decisions should capture full context, options, risks, and consequences.

faq.question: Can I edit an existing ADR?
faq.answer: No. ADRs are immutable historical records. If circumstances change, create a new ADR that supersedes the old one with a reference to the original.

faq.question: What belongs in the Consequences section?
faq.answer: Document positive benefits, negative costs, identified risks with mitigations, and reversibility assessment (Easy, Moderate, Difficult, or Irreversible).

faq.question: How do I number my ADRs?
faq.answer: Use sequential numbering like ADR-001, ADR-002. Include the number in the filename and title for easy reference and linking between related decisions.
